answers.value: Most consistent times are Monday, Wednesday, and Friday mornings between 9:00 a.m. and 12:00 p.m. I can also occasionally cover a Saturday afternoon (about twice a month) if there is a special event or an outing that needs extra volunteers, as long as I have at least a week’s notice.

answers.value: My initial commitment is for 12 months, but if things are going well for both the community and me, I would be very open to continuing on an ongoing basis.

answers.field_label: Why Are You Interested In Volunteering At The Waters
answers.value:
I am interested in volunteering at The Waters because I have seen firsthand how much of a difference consistent, caring human connection can make in the lives of seniors. My mother, Carol, lived in a senior community in Wisconsin for the last five years of her life, and the volunteers there became an extension of our family. I still remember one volunteer, a retired teacher named June, who came every Wednesday afternoon just to play Scrabble and listen to my mom’s stories about growing up on a farm. Those visits improved my mom’s mood dramatically, gave her something to look forward to, and took a lot of emotional pressure off of me as her daughter and caregiver.

Over the last few years, my own children have grown more independent (our youngest just started college), and I’ve found that I have both the time and emotional bandwidth to give back in a more structured way. I work part‑time as a bookkeeper from home, which gives me a flexible schedule, and I’ve been looking for a way to use that flexibility to do something meaningful outside my immediate family. I’m drawn specifically to The Waters because of your emphasis on community, dignity, and engagement rather than just ‘caregiving.’ I appreciate that The Waters talks about residents as people with histories, preferences, and personalities, not just as patients or room numbers.

On a personal level, I genuinely enjoy intergenerational connections. I’ve always gravitated toward older adults—at church, I tend to sit with and chat with the older members, and in my neighborhood I’m the one who checks in on the widowed gentleman down the block. I like hearing people’s life stories, and I find that seniors appreciate being treated as equals in conversation rather than being talked down to or rushed. I’m also very patient by nature, which I know is important when someone moves more slowly, needs extra time to find words, or is having a day where they’re feeling confused or frustrated.

I also see this as a way to model community involvement for my own family. My husband and I have talked a lot about wanting to age in a community where people look out for one another. Volunteering at The Waters feels like a concrete way to help build that kind of environment now, for people who need it today, while also learning what good, person‑centered senior care looks like for the future. In short, I’m looking not just to ‘help out’ occasionally, but to become a familiar, reliable presence for residents—someone they recognize, trust, and can count on to show up consistently and with a warm, calm attitude.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Areas Of Interest With Residents Skills And Previous Experience Volunteering With Seniors
answers.value:
Areas of interest with residents:
- One‑on‑one visits and friendly conversations: I really enjoy sitting down with someone, asking open‑ended questions about their life, and listening closely. I’m especially interested in visiting residents who may not get many outside visitors or who are feeling isolated. I’m comfortable with quiet moments and don’t feel the need to ‘fill the silence,’ which I’ve found can be reassuring for some seniors.
- Reading aloud and letter writing: My mother’s eyesight declined significantly in her last years, and I used to read to her—novels, devotionals, newspaper articles, even her old letters. I’d love to do similar reading for residents who enjoy books or magazines but have difficulty seeing the print. I also enjoy writing and would be happy to help residents write cards or emails to family members, or even record their memories in a simple ‘life story’ format.
- Small group activities: I like light‑hearted, low‑pressure group activities such as bingo, simple crafts, trivia, or board and card games. I play a lot of Rummikub and Uno with my own family and have led bingo and trivia at my church’s senior luncheon.
- Music and reminiscence: I’m not a professional musician, but I do play basic piano and know a number of older hymns and standards (e.g., ‘You Are My Sunshine,’ ‘Let Me Call You Sweetheart,’ ‘Amazing Grace’). I would be comfortable helping with sing‑alongs if needed or just using music as a way to connect and reminisce.

Skills and relevant background:
- Communication and patience: In addition to supporting my mother and my late father‑in‑law as they aged, I have spent many years volunteering in intergenerational settings. I’m a good, calm communicator and I’m used to repeating information gently, speaking clearly, and being attentive to body language. I understand that some residents may have hearing loss, memory issues, or dementia, and I try to approach every interaction with respect, humor, and zero judgment.
- Experience with seniors: 
  • Personal caregiving: From 2015 to 2020, I helped coordinate my mother’s care while she lived in an assisted living community in Eau Claire, WI. Although I was not a paid caregiver, I visited most weekends, accompanied her to appointments, advocated for her with staff, and helped with personal tasks like organizing her room, sorting her mail, and managing her calendar.
  • Church volunteer work: For the past seven years, I’ve volunteered with the ‘Senior Connections’ ministry at Grace Lutheran Church in Minnetonka. This has included monthly visits to homebound members (typically women in their late 70s to early 90s), bringing small care packages, reading scripture or devotionals, and simply spending time chatting. During the early part of the COVID‑19 pandemic, I made weekly phone calls to about eight seniors from our congregation to check in, provide social contact, and help coordinate grocery or prescription deliveries with other volunteers.
  • Community events: I have helped organize and staff an annual ‘Senior Appreciation Luncheon’ at our church, which typically hosts 60–80 older adults from the community. My roles have included greeting guests, assisting people with mobility challenges to find seating, serving food, leading a short trivia game, and helping with clean‑up. Through these events, I’ve learned to be attentive to dietary restrictions, mobility aids, and the pacing of activities so that no one feels rushed or left out.
- Practical skills: Professionally, I work part‑time as a bookkeeper for a small landscaping company, so I’m organized, detail‑oriented, and comfortable following procedures and maintaining confidentiality. I’m reliable about schedules and communication—if I commit to coming, I show up on time, and if something unexpected happens, I give as much notice as possible.
- Personality and temperament: People who know me would describe me as warm, steady, and unflappable. I don’t mind repeating myself, I don’t take impatience personally, and I try to find small bits of humor and joy even on difficult days. I’m also very comfortable with differences—whether that’s cultural background, religious beliefs, or cognitive ability—and I make a conscious effort to meet people where they are.

Previous experience specifically volunteering with seniors:
While I have not formally volunteered with The Waters before, I have roughly a decade of informal and formal experience with seniors:
- 2013–2016: Volunteer visitor with ‘Neighbors Helping Neighbors’ in Eau Claire, WI (through my mother’s community). I visited two residents regularly, one with early‑stage dementia and one who was largely bed‑bound due to mobility issues. I would read, help them write notes to family members, and occasionally accompany them to on‑site events.
- 2017–present: Senior Connections volunteer at Grace Lutheran Church (Minnetonka, MN), including home visits, phone check‑ins, and helping coordinate transportation for seniors to church events.

Overall, I feel comfortable and energized in senior‑living environments and am eager to learn The Waters’ specific policies and best practices so I can support residents and staff in a way that is truly helpful and appropriate.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
